text.title: From Hourly to Fixed-Price Transition Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every application of `From Hourly to Fixed-Price Transition` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: First conversion MUST be the highest-margin existing client. Margin = current monthly revenue / operator hours, compared to target effective rate.
rule.rationale: First conversion has the highest emotional cost (fear of loss). Highest-margin client = lowest financial + emotional risk because you're already over-earning.

rule.statement: MUST maintain 2-3 hourly clients during pilot. Convert at most ONE client per month during the first quarter.
rule.rationale: Hourly clients are the cash floor while fixed-price economics stabilize. All-at-once converts create 4-8 week trough few solos survive.

rule.statement: Identify exactly ONE repeatable scope (audit / migration / integration / sprint) appearing in >= 3 past engagements. Productize that scope only — no second SKU in the same quarter.
rule.rationale: Multiple new SKUs split positioning, marketing, and operator focus. One sharp SKU outsells a menu of three for the first 6-12 months.

rule.statement: After pilot closes, re-price ALL remaining hourly clients in a single one-week communication window. Staggered re-pricing forbidden.
rule.rationale: Staggered re-pricing leaks across the client network ('Why did Acme get $X and I got $Y?'). One round, same effective date = fairness + finality.

rule.statement: Before starting transition, document a 6-8 week cash bridge: savings + accessible credit + signed pipeline within trough window. Bridge < 6 weeks forbidden.
rule.rationale: Operators forced to abort mid-transition (cash, not strategy) end up worse than where they started. Bridge is non-negotiable.

rule.statement: When preconditions in Applies If fail OR any item in Skip If triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
